text: The patient is positioned on their left side with the right flank exposed, preparing for the nephrectomy procedure.
The surgeon positions the left hand operating port at a subcostal site along the midclavicular line, visible through the camera port.
The surgeon positions the right hand operating port three finger breadths supramedial to the anterior superior iliac spine, preparing for the nephrectomy procedure.
The camera port is positioned using a Hassan cut-down technique, triangulated between the right hand operating port and the left hand operating port, visible in the surgical field.

Depending upon the body mass index of the patient, the position of the camera port will be variable and the umbilicus is not used as a landmark unless in the very slim patient an umbilical camera port is required.
The surgeon observes the benign non-functioning kidney, containing multiple large cysts, prolapsing through the mesentery medial to the descending colon.
However, the first steps of surgery are the same regardless of the pathology for which we're operating on the left side.
The surgeon makes an initial incision in the posterior peritoneum, preparing to access the underlying structures.
The surgeon makes a superficial incision in the peritoneum, carefully avoiding the fascial layer that covers the durotus fat.

The surgeon preserves the fascial layer during dissection, facilitating a more straightforward separation of tissue planes around the kidney, which is visible prolapsing through the mesentery medial to the descending colon.
The surgeon mobilizes the colon to create space anterior to the prolapsed kidney, which is visible through the mesentery medial to the descending colon.
The surgeon reflects the colon medially by pulling the mesocolic fat forward along with the colon, creating space for further dissection.
The surgeon accesses a critical point to reflect the colon, facilitating the exposure of the surgical field for the nephrectomy procedure.
The surgeon carefully dissects through the mesocolic fat, avoiding deeper penetration to prevent loss of anatomical orientation and minimize bleeding from vessels within the mesocolon.
The surgeon reflects the mesocolic fat medially while attempting to preserve the integrity of Girota's fascia, creating a clear view of the anatomical planes.
The surgeon develops the anatomical plane necessary for a left-sided nephrectomy, focusing on creating space anterior to the kidney by mobilizing the colon and reflecting mesocolic fat medially.
The surgeon carefully develops the surgical plane atraumatically, ensuring minimal disruption to surrounding tissues during the left-sided nephrectomy.
The surgeon identifies holes within the fascia overlying Girota's fat and adjusts the dissection site to avoid compromising the fascia integrity.
In patients with inflammatory conditions of the kidney, such as previous infection or previous bleeding, and indeed in TCC, this plane can be challenging.
The surgeon uses the left hand to lift and retract the mesocolon superiorly and laterally, facilitating the identification of the gonadal vein and ureter.
The surgeon begins dissecting the ureter adjacent to the lower pole of the left kidney, following the identification of the anatomical plane.

The surgeon identifies the ureter adjacent to the lower pole of the left kidney and confirms its identity. The left hand is placed beneath the ureter to lift it laterally, facilitating further dissection.
The surgeon creates space to clarify and divide the tissue between locking clips, facilitating further dissection.
The surgeon identifies the gonadal vein lying lateral to the ureter and divides it between locking clips.
The surgeon continues the dissection of the gonadal vein using a harmonic energy device, facilitating a seamless division of the vein without the necessity of the device.
After dividing the vein, the surgeon continues dissection on the lateral aspect of the kidney, detaching it from the abdominal wall.
The surgeon completes the division of the ureter, ensuring it is no longer a focus in the surgical field.
following the ureter to the renal hilum is a technique that should be consigned to history since this takes us to a point on the hilum that is far too lateral for safe hilar dissection.
The ureter is visibly divided and then left undisturbed in the surgical field.
The surgeon reflects the colon medially, moving it away from the anterior surface of the kidney to expose the surgical field for further dissection.
The surgeon encounters challenges in detaching the kidney from the abdominal wall due to its enlarged size, which is attributed to large, previously complicated renal cysts.
The surgeon uses a left hand retractor to apply tension, carefully lifting the posterior surface of the colon away from the anterior surface of the kidney while preserving fascial layers.
The surgeon approaches the splenic flexure of the colon, noting its lateral arching, while carefully monitoring both sides of the dissection to avoid contact with the energy device.
The surgeon reflects the posterior surface of the colon off the entire length of the kidney, addressing any adhesions between the peritoneum and the fascia overlaying Girota's fat.
The surgeon lifts the lower pole of the kidney laterally, revealing the anatomical plane that will be followed to access the renal hilum.
The surgeon addresses further adhesions on the posterior surface of the colon, carefully separating them from the underlying structures.
It's important for me to emphasise the role of the left hand, which is a strong retractor and does most of the work in the dissection by applying tension to the layers we wish to identify and divide.
The surgical setup for a left-sided nephrectomy is shown, utilizing three ports: one for the camera and two for operating instruments.

In cases requiring radical dissection of the renal hilum, such as renal cancer surgery with lymphadenopathy or live donor nephrectomy, a fourth port is placed laterally to push the colon medially, exposing additional veins and arteries.
The surgeon lifts the lower pole of the kidney laterally, dividing the fascia overlying it to further reflect the tissue and expose the inferior margin of the renal hilum.
The surgeon performs dissection medial to the ureter's course, intentionally bypassing the ureter as a landmark during the procedure.
The surgeon explores the dissection plane, lifting the kidney laterally while the colon is moved medially, facilitating exposure of the renal hilum.
The surgeon observes a pulsation within the mesocolon at the seven o'clock position in the image, indicating the presence of a vascular structure.
The surgeon mobilizes the colon further to provide clear access to the renal hilum, facilitating exposure of the vein and artery.
The surgeon separates adhesions between the posterior surface of the splenic flexure and the superior margin of the kidney, revealing the anatomical structures involved in the nephrectomy procedure.
It's important at this phase of the dissection to check intermittently that the spleen is not being approached through the
The surgeon applies traction and counter traction to atraumatically separate tissue planes, facilitating the lateral movement of the kidney and medial displacement of the colon.
The surgeon uses an energy device to manage and separate adhesions visible around the surgical field.

The surgeon expresses caution while dissecting near the posterior surface of the splenic flexure of the colon, which is positioned behind the left hand retractor. The spleen is located just behind the fascial layer currently being cleaned.
It's not routine to detach the spleen from the lateral abdominal wall.
I don't believe this increases in any way the mobilization and the view during a left-sided nephrectomy.
The surgeon divides adhesions overlaying the renal hilum, using surgical instruments to carefully separate these tissue connections.
The surgeon divides the thin fascial layer overlying Gerota's fascia to gain access to the renal hilar vessels, visible at the base of the image.
The surgeon encounters the gonadal vein, which is visible running from the one o'clock to the seven o'clock position in the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel, resembling a diathermy hook, to expose and divide the very thin fascial layer overlying the renal hilum.
The surgeon sweeps away the fat overlying the renal hilum, revealing the renal vein for further dissection.
The surgeon uses the harmonic scalpel to dissect parallel to the expected direction of the vein, carefully maneuvering the instrument along the tissue planes.
If that plane of sweeping were perpendicular, it would increase the risk of damage to delicate venous structure.
The surgeon carefully dissects the anterior surface of the renal vein, ensuring that accessory veins draining into the renal vein remain intact and undisturbed.
The surgeon performs suction dissection parallel to the anterior surface of the renal vein, carefully maneuvering to avoid disrupting accessory veins.
The surgeon exposes the inferior edge of the left renal vein, revealing its anatomical position within the surgical field.
At this point it's crucial only to divide tissue that you can see all the way through.
This is because of a crucial tributary to the left renal vein, the lumbar vein.
The surgeon divides the fascia overlying the renal vein, allowing for the exposure of the underlying anatomical structures.
The surgeon exposes the insertion point of the lumbar vein into the inferior margin of the left renal vein, highlighting the anatomical connection between these vascular structures.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to point at and divide the fascia overlying the lumbar vein, revealing the underlying anatomical structure.
The surgeon identifies the anatomical triangle formed between the medial aspect of the lumbar vein and the inferior margin of the left renal vein, ensuring precise visualization of these structures.
The surgeon performs a radical dissection of the hilar structures, focusing on the division of the lumbar vein to ensure proper exposure and separation from the left renal vein.
As will be seen later, this allows access to the renal artery in an uncomplicated manner.
The surgeon uses irrigation to free adherent fascia from the surface of the lumbar vein, revealing the vein's structure beneath.
The surgeon uses an instrument to slowly expose the tributaries of the vein, ensuring atraumatic separation of the surrounding fascia.
The surgeon lifts the vein anteriorly to gain access to the renal artery, revealing the anatomical pathway for further dissection.

If we'd followed the ureter to the hilum, this dissection would have been more challenging and the renal artery would already have divided into its triple branches, making arterial division more complex.

Equally, if the gonadal vein is followed to the renal vein, our dissection would be one or two centimetres more lateral and this does not routinely include the lymph node packet that's associated with the left renal artery.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to open the anatomical triangle located between the lumbar vein and the renal vein, revealing the space between these vascular structures.
The surgeon prepares to access and divide the lumbar vein, following the exposure of the vein's tributaries.
The surgeon applies constant tension with the left hand to maintain safety during the dissection, ensuring precise manipulation of the anatomical structures.
The surgeon uses the harmonic scalpel to apply heat energy, ensuring that tension lifts the fascia away from the underlying vascular structures to prevent damage during dissection.
You'll note that during this whole dissection, the camera has remained within the patient and has not been removed for cleaning.
If the camera becomes splattered with fat or blood, then cleaning with the suction irrigation whilst the camera remains in situ is more than adequate.
Constant removal and reinsertion of the camera for cleaning delays the procedure, and if it's done during a time of active bleeding, can in fact lead to harm.
by delaying the control of any bleeding.
The renal vein is visible, oriented vertically from twelve to six o'clock, with the lumbar vein branching off laterally.
The surgeon observes the pulsation of the aorta, which is visible running diagonally from the five o'clock position to the eleven o'clock position within the surgical field.
The aorta is visually encircled by two veins, with the renal vein positioned vertically and the lumbar vein extending laterally.
The surgeon carefully cleans the lumbar vein, preparing it for division between locking clips.
A surgical clip is applied around the vein at a convenient position, securing it effectively.
The surgical clip is partially closed and slid into position around the renal vein, preparing it for fixation.
This slide usually reduces the amount of perivascular adhesions that can get trapped within the clip and allow mobility of the vessel once it's been divided.
After dividing the lumbar vein, the renal artery becomes visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon approaches a single renal artery that has not yet branched, preparing for further dissection or division.
The surgeon approaches the renal artery as it emerges from the wall of the aorta, preparing for further dissection or ligation.
The surgeon performs a radical dissection during the donor nephrectomy, aiming to maximize the length of the renal artery for transplantation.
The renal artery is visibly located on the lateral side of the vein, clearly distinguishable in the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a right-angle instrument to create a space, ensuring the pointed tips are directed away from the renal vein, maintaining a safe distance from the vessel.
The surgeon proceeds to divide the renal artery after creating an appropriate space, ensuring the artery is separated from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon applies a staple line to the renal artery for secure division, supplementing the locking clips previously placed.
The surgeon uses a linear stapler to place three rows of staples on the renal artery, securing it for division.
The surgeon examines the application line of the staples on the renal artery, ensuring good control before proceeding with its division.
The surgeon approaches the renal artery from the right-hand port, positioning the clips parallel to the aorta for secure application.
The surgeon places additional locking clips medial to the staple line on the patient's side and lateral to the staple line on the specimen's side, securing the artery for division.
The surgeon safely divides the artery after securing it with a staple line and additional locking clips.
The surgeon prepares to divide the renal vein, following the placement of three rows of staples on the artery.
The surgeon has created a space above the renal vein, preparing to apply a clip to secure the vessel before division.
The adrenal vein is visible entering the superior margin of the renal vein, observed at the lower part of the surgical window.
A row of staples is applied across the tissue, followed by the placement of additional locking clips for secure closure.
The surgeon continues the dissection, revealing the adrenal gland at the inferior margin of the surgical field.

The surgeon continues the dissection line superiorly and lateral to the adrenal gland, ensuring the adrenal vein remains intact unless performing a radical nephrectomy, which would involve dividing the adrenal vein.
The adrenal gland is visually included with the nephrectomy specimen, indicating its removal along with the kidney.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to divide tissue just lateral to the visible slither of the adrenal vein, which is located at the superior margin of the renal vein.
The surgeon has divided the hilum, lateral attachments, and some anterior attachments of the kidney, preparing for its removal during the nephrectomy procedure.
The kidney is still attached by adhesions in the superior medial groove, visible as fibrous tissue connections that need to be carefully dissected.
The surgeon divides the remaining attachments of the kidney, choosing the direction based on the kidney's position, either from above or inferiorly.
The surgeon alternates between superior and inferior views to mobilize the kidney, adjusting the approach to facilitate its detachment.
The surgeon uses the harmonic scalpel near the pancreas and splenic vein, visible just beyond the tip of the instrument.
The surgeon applies lateral retraction with the left hand to place the adhesions under tension, providing a clear view for safe dissection of the kidney.
The surgeon performs lateral dissection near the kidney, taking care to avoid incising too close to the organ.
The surgeon creates a plane of dissection further away from the kidney, incorporating Gerota's fascia and surrounding fat into the specimen, ensuring a safer separation from the kidney.
The surgeon continues dissection in a less vascular plane, maintaining a clear view of the anatomical structures surrounding the kidney.
It's also oncologically more sound.
Whilst the dissection continues both medially and laterally, I'll point out some of the key factors in our enhanced recovery programme.
We don't routinely place a renal drain.
We don't routinely place a urethral catheter.

All patients are counselled about this in anticipation of their surgery and are consented for the approximately ten percent chance of requiring a single episode of intermittent self-catheterisation post-op.
Patients are mobilised on the evening of surgery and they're given free access to whatever diet and fluid they choose after the operation.
The chewing of gum in the post-operative day can reduce the risk of post-operative ileus if the patient has not returned to full diet.
We aim for discharge the day following surgery.

The expectation of discharge on day one is set at the first meeting in the outpatient clinic and the messages around the enhanced recovery programme are reinforced each time the patient is met and again at the time of consent.
The surgeon divides the final adhesions at the upper pole of the kidney, completing the separation of the kidney from surrounding tissues.
The importance of traction is clear to see in exposing the plane of division.
The surgeon carefully divides tissue close to the kidney, mindful of the tail of the pancreas located just behind the fascia on the left side.
The surgeon reveals the nearly freed kidney, allowing the size and dimensions of the specimen to be visually appreciated.
The surgeon completes the division of the final adhesion at the upper pole, fully freeing the kidney specimen from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon inspects the surgical field for any bleeding points and uses an energy device to control them, ensuring hemostasis after the kidney has been freed from adhesions.
The surgeon applies a clip to achieve control, particularly if the adrenal gland has been transgressed during the dissection, as initial control with energy was not sufficient.
The surgeon inspects the hilum of the kidney, addressing any visible points of bleeding using appropriate hemostatic techniques.
The surgeon places the kidney back into the renal bed and inserts an endoscopic bag into the abdomen to facilitate the delivery of the specimen.
The surgeon uses an endoscopic bag to scoop the kidney specimen, avoiding the use of a retractor to lift it, ensuring smooth placement into the bag.
The surgeon extends the left iliac fossa port to deliver the kidney specimen from the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon performs a final inspection of the surgical field, checking for any obvious bleeding points around the hilum and other dissected areas.
prior to specimen delivery and wound closure.
The operation is then complete.
